summary: Science review + authoritative data reconnaissance for lunar eclipse turquoise band reproduction

reviews.angle: Phenomenology and physical cause of the turquoise/blue band (turquoise fringe) on the eclipsed Moon during total lunar eclipses, with quantitative observational and spectroscopic confirmation that ozone Chappuis-band absorption is the accepted explanation.
key_findings: WHAT IS OBSERVED: A thin band/fringe of turquoise-to-blue (teal, blue-green) color seen along the INNER edge of Earth's umbral shadow on the lunar disk, i.e. on the part of the Moon nearest the umbra boundary/terminator, bordering the copper-red interior. NASA's Visual Appearance page describes the outer, brightest part of the umbra (Danjon L=3-4 eclipses) as having 'a bluish, very bright rim'; the tadst.com 'Handy Guide' explicitly notes a 'turquoise/bluish edge' for copper-red/orange eclipses. Source: eclipse.gsfc.nasa.gov/LEcat5/appearance.html; c.tadst.com/gfx/pdf/infographic--total-lunar-eclipses.pdf.

STAGE/TIMING: It is a transient feature best seen in the FIRST and LAST minutes of totality (just before totality begins and just after it ends), when part of the Moon is near the umbra edge. Richard Keen (Univ. Colorado), quoted via NASA: 'look during the first and last minutes of totality. The turquoise rim is best seen in binoculars or a small telescope.' PBS: 'if the skies are clear enough and you look at just the right moment, you might glimpse a band of turquoise.' It is a difficult, marginal naked-eye target ('you have to be a black-belt eclipse watcher to see it' -- Tony Phillips). Sources: gizmodo.com/why-you-might-see-turquoise-...-1641223349; pbs.org/newshour/science/ozone-challenge-can-see-turquoise-lunar-eclipse.

WHERE ON THE DISK / GEOMETRY: The turquoise appears at the umbra EDGE, not the center. The red core comes from sunlight that has skimmed through the lower troposphere (heavily Rayleigh-reddened); the blue/turquoise edge comes from rays that passed HIGHER, through the stratospheric ozone layer (15-40 km tangent altitude). Cloudy Nights (quoting the physics): 'light near the edge of the umbra (~42 arc min from umbra center) appears turquoise or blue because it has passed well above the troposphere.' The color thus maps to ray tangent height: low tangent height -> red center, high tangent height (above ~tropopause, into ozone layer) -> blue/turquoise rim. Source: cloudynights.com/forums/topic/513753; eclipse.gsfc.nasa.gov/LEcat5/appearance.html.

COLOR/HUE: Described consistently as turquoise, teal, blue-green, or soft/pale blue ('a soft blue fringe around the red core' -- Keen). It is genuinely blue-green rather than pure blue, reflecting partial (not total) red removal. Sources: gizmodo (Keen); linkedin.com/pulse/blood-moon-turquoise-fringe-...zheng.

WIDTH: A NARROW band -- variously 'a slim, cool ribbon along the inner edge,' 'a narrow teal band,' 'a thin band of turquoise.' No single canonical angular width is quoted in the popular literature (it depends on how steeply the ray-tangent-height-to-color gradient maps onto the umbra edge and on ozone column); reports emphasize it is a thin fringe/rim a small fraction of the lunar radius wide, sharpest right at the umbra boundary. Sources: linkedin (Zheng); pbs; gizmodo.

ACCEPTED PHYSICAL EXPLANATION = OZONE CHAPPUIS ABSORPTION: CONFIRMED. NASA (via Keen): 'The source of the turquoise is ozone... light passing through the upper stratosphere penetrates the ozone layer, which absorbs red light and actually makes the passing light ray bluer.' PBS: 'That turquoise is caused by the ozone in the upper atmosphere, which blocks out the red light and enhances the blue.' Mechanism: rays grazing at stratospheric tangent heights traverse a long ozone column; the Chappuis band absorbs orange-red light (broad, max near 600 nm), leaving residual blue-green light, which is refracted into the umbra's edge. Sources: gizmodo; pbs; linkedin.

SPECTROSCOPIC / PEER-REVIEWED CONFIRMATION: (1) ESO Messenger No.143 (Fosbury & Koch, 2011) -- the weak visible Chappuis band 'becomes an important influence on the colour of the entire sky when the Sun is low or just below the horizon,' explored 'using spectra of the sunset and also of the eclipsed Moon'; ozone 'absorbing across the entire visible spectrum with a maximum close to 600 nm'; 'By absorbing red and orange light, the Chappuis band has the effect of imparting a pale blue colour to pure ozone gas.' (2) Garcia-Munoz & Palle, simulations of the 2007/2008 total lunar eclipses using ray tracing with refraction, molecular scattering, aerosols and OZONE reproduce observed eclipse color (Appl. Opt. 47, H149; pubmed 19037336). (3) Palle et al. 2009 Earth transmission spectrum from lunar eclipses: continuum shaped by 'ozone Chappuis band absorption between 0.375 and 0.650 microns' plus Rayleigh scattering. Sources: eso.org/.../messenger-no143-27-31.pdf; opg.optica.org/abstract.cfm?uri=ao-47-34-h149; research.iac.es/.../Palle_2009_earth_transm.pdf.

WHY OZONE (not just Rayleigh) MATTERS: Rayleigh scattering alone reddens transmitted light monotonically and cannot produce a blue rim; it would make even high rays reddish-orange. The distinct turquoise REQUIRES a second absorber that preferentially removes red/orange at high tangent heights where Rayleigh optical depth is small but the ozone column is still large. The Chappuis band (a broad absorption ~450-700 nm peaking ~575-603 nm) is that absorber. This is the discriminating evidence that the band is an ozone signature, not a refraction/Rayleigh artifact. NASA's appearance page describes the bluish rim purely via refraction-layering and notably does NOT mention ozone, but the spectroscopic literature and Keen/Phillips attribute the specific turquoise hue to ozone -- both are operative (refraction sets WHERE high rays land; ozone sets the blue-green COLOR).
reviews.physics_model_recommendation: Model the umbral illumination as a sum over refracted solar rays parameterized by their tangent (perigee) height h above Earth's surface; each ray's tangent height maps to a radial position in the umbra (low h -> deep in umbra/center, high h -> umbra edge). For each ray compute the transmitted spectrum I(lambda) = I_sun(lambda) * exp(-tau_total(lambda, h)), where tau_total = tau_Rayleigh + tau_aerosol + tau_O3(Chappuis) + tau_clouds, each integrated along the tangential (limb) path through the spherical-shell atmosphere (Chapman-function / long-slant-path geometry). Use: Rayleigh tau ~ lambda^-4 weighted by air column along the slant path (dominant for low h, in troposphere); ozone Chappuis cross-section sigma_O3(lambda) (broad, peak ~600 nm, ~4.5e-21 cm^2/molecule at peak) times the slant O3 column (ozone concentrated 15-40 km, so it dominates the optical path precisely for rays with tangent heights ~20-35 km that form the umbra edge). Refraction must be included to (a) bend rays into the umbra at all and (b) set the h->umbra-radius mapping and differential refraction across the disk. Recommended approach: the Garcia-Munoz & Palle (2008, Appl. Opt. 47, H149) ray-tracing framework is the validated reference. The turquoise emerges naturally as the residual blue-green of high-tangent-height rays once Chappuis removes the orange-red. SAFE approximations: spherically symmetric atmosphere; treat Sun as point or simple limb-darkened disk; exponential air density; standard mid-latitude ozone profile (~300 DU). DANGEROUS approximations: (1) omitting refraction entirely -- no umbra illumination and wrong radial color mapping; (2) omitting ozone -- you lose the turquoise completely (this is the whole phenomenon); (3) treating ozone as a uniform slab rather than altitude-resolved -- the band's edge-localization depends on ozone being a stratospheric layer; (4) ignoring tropospheric clouds/aerosols around the terminator -- they strongly modulate visibility and Danjon brightness, hence whether the fringe is seen at all.
reviews.expected_quantitative_signature: Turquoise/blue appears at the umbra EDGE, mapping to rays with high tangent heights -- physically rays passing through and above the ozone layer (ozone 15-40 km; the strongest ozone-blue contribution comes from tangent heights roughly 20-35 km where the slant O3 column is large but Rayleigh column is modest). In angular terms a Cloudy Nights physics summary places the turquoise transition near ~42 arc-minutes from the umbra CENTER (i.e. close to the umbral radius, which is ~40-46 arc-min depending on the eclipse) -- consistent with 'edge of the umbra.' Spectral signature: the Chappuis absorption band is broad over ~450-700 nm with absorption maximum near 575-603 nm (commonly cited ~600 nm; ESO Messenger says 'maximum close to 600 nm'; the LinkedIn piece narrows the visually relevant notch to 'roughly 575-603 nm'). Removing this orange-red notch from an otherwise reddened limb spectrum shifts the residual color toward blue-green (hue ~480-510 nm dominant, hence 'turquoise' rather than pure blue ~450 nm). Quantitatively, pure ozone gas itself is pale blue from Chappuis absorption -- the laboratory analog cited in the ESO Messenger. Visibility/strength scales with stratospheric ozone column (Keen has used eclipse brightness/color as a proxy for stratospheric aerosol and ozone): more ozone => stronger, more saturated turquoise. The feature is a thin rim (small fraction of lunar radius) and is best-seen in the first/last ~1-3 minutes of totality. Sources: eso.org messenger-no143; cloudynights.com/forums/topic/513753; linkedin.com/pulse/blood-moon-turquoise-fringe; Palle_2009 (Chappuis 0.375-0.650 um).
pitfalls: Do NOT model the color as pure blue -- it is turquoise/blue-green because the red is only partially removed and some longer-wavelength light remains; producing saturated blue means you over-applied the absorber or omitted residual Rayleigh-transmitted red.

Do NOT attribute the band solely to Rayleigh scattering. Rayleigh alone reddens all transmitted rays and cannot make a blue rim; the discriminating cause of the HUE is ozone Chappuis absorption. Conversely, do not attribute it solely to ozone and ignore refraction -- refraction is what places high-tangent-height rays at the umbra edge in the first place.

Watch the geometry direction: turquoise is at the umbra EDGE / inner shadow boundary (the limb of the eclipsed Moon nearest the bright/uneclipsed side), NOT at the umbra center. The center is the darkest, reddest part. Getting center/edge inverted inverts the whole color curve.

Ozone must be treated as a stratospheric LAYER (15-40 km), not a uniform column. The band's spatial localization at the umbra edge depends on the fact that high-tangent-height rays accumulate a disproportionately large ozone slant column while having a small Rayleigh column. A well-mixed-absorber model will smear the color and fail to localize the fringe.

Visibility is highly variable eclipse-to-eclipse and depends on stratospheric aerosols (e.g. post-volcanic loading darkens and reddens, can suppress the fringe), cloud distribution around Earth's terminator, and ozone column. A model predicting an always-vivid band contradicts observations -- many eclipses show it 'barely at all.' Treat it as marginal/threshold by design.

NASA's official Visual Appearance page explains the bluish rim via refraction/atmospheric layering and does NOT mention ozone; some secondary sources echo only the refraction story. Relying on that page alone would under-specify the cause. The ozone-Chappuis attribution comes from Keen/Phillips (NASA outreach) and the peer-reviewed spectroscopy (ESO Messenger, Garcia-Munoz & Palle, Palle 2009) -- cite those for the ozone mechanism.

The 'best seen first/last minutes of totality' timing is because only then is a portion of the disk at the right radial position near the umbra edge under favorable refracted illumination; a static mid-totality model may show little turquoise even when correct.
reviews.confidence: High. The phenomenology (turquoise fringe at the umbra edge, first/last minutes of totality, thin band, blue-green hue) is consistently reported across independent observational/outreach sources (NASA via Keen, PBS via Phillips, Sky&Telescope-adjacent guides, Cloudy Nights), and the ozone-Chappuis explanation is independently confirmed by peer-reviewed spectroscopy of the eclipsed Moon as a proxy for Earth's transmission spectrum (ESO Messenger No.143 Fosbury & Koch 2011; Garcia-Munoz & Palle Appl. Opt. 47 H149 2008; Palle et al. 2009). Quantitative ozone band peak (~600 nm) and altitude (15-40 km) are well established. The one soft spot is the precise canonical angular WIDTH of the band, which the popular literature gives only qualitatively ('thin'/'narrow'); deriving it requires running the ray-tracing/Chappuis model (the ~42 arc-min figure is the edge LOCATION, not the width).

reviews.angle: Ozone Chappuis-band absorption physics as the cause of the turquoise rim on a totally eclipsed Moon: cross-section spectrum, the limb (tangent-path) geometry that amplifies a weak vertical optical depth into a color-selective filter, and the quantitative optical-depth budget across the visible.
key_findings: CHAPPUIS BAND BASICS: A broad continuum absorption of ozone in the visible, caused by photodissociation of O3. Commonly cited range ~400-650 nm (Wikipedia/Chappuis absorption), with the spectroscopic community (JPL Data Evaluation, IUP-Bremen) treating it as part of a 370-790 nm system. Two twin maxima: a primary peak at ~602-603 nm and a secondary at ~575 nm; effective center of the absorption dip in twilight/eclipse spectra sits around 585-600 nm.

PEAK CROSS-SECTION: sigma(603 nm) = 5.23e-21 cm^2/molecule; sigma(575 nm) = 4.83e-21 cm^2/molecule (Wikipedia citing standard lab data; JPL Sec.4 confirms ~5.2e-21 near 602 nm with inter-dataset agreement within a few %). This is ~3 orders of magnitude WEAKER than the UV Hartley band (~1.1e-17 cm^2 at 255 nm) - which is exactly why it only becomes visible over very long ozone paths. Absorption is nearly temperature-independent (<3% variation), so a single 298 K cross-section is adequate.

TURQUOISE MECHANISM: Chappuis absorption preferentially removes orange-red light (560-650 nm) while leaving blue-green (450-520 nm) and far-red (>680 nm) relatively untouched. Pure ozone gas in the lab looks pale blue for this reason. During totality, the deep-red umbra core is set by Rayleigh/Mie extinction in the dense lower troposphere (short wavelengths scattered out -> red transmitted), but the OUTER umbra fringe is lit by sunlight refracted through the higher, thinner stratosphere where the dominant remaining absorber is ozone; there the Chappuis band carves out the orange-red and the rim turns turquoise/blue (Vollmer & Gedzelman, Applied Optics 47, H149, 2008).

VERTICAL OZONE COLUMN: Global mean ~300 DU. 1 DU = 2.69e16 molecules/cm^2, so 300 DU = 8.07e18 molecules/cm^2 (~3 mm of pure O3 at STP). This is the VERTICAL column; the eclipse/twilight geometry multiplies it.

VERTICAL OPTICAL DEPTH IS TINY: tau_vertical(603nm) = sigma * N = 5.2e-21 * 8.07e18 ~ 0.042 (transmission 96%). Looking straight up through the ozone layer you would barely notice Chappuis absorption - this is the key point: the effect is ONLY visible because of the enormously long limb path.

LIMB/TANGENT-PATH ENHANCEMENT IS THE WHOLE STORY: Light reaching the umbra grazes Earth tangentially. A ray with tangent height near/below the ozone layer (peak ~22 km, layer ~15-35 km) traverses a horizontal chord of order 600-700 km through ozone-rich air. Geometric slant/vertical air-mass factor (AMF) is large: a simple spherical-shell chord through a 15-35 km layer at 12 km tangent height gives AMF ~ 35; an exponential-layer estimate sqrt(pi*R/H) gives AMF ~ 45-50 for H~8-10 km. Take AMF ~ 12-45 depending on tangent altitude.

RESULTING LIMB OPTICAL DEPTH IN CHAPPUIS BAND: With AMF~35 and 300 DU, slant column ~2.8e20 molecules/cm^2 and tau(603nm) ~ 1.5 (transmission ~23%); at AMF~45, tau ~1.9 (T~15%). Even a conservative AMF~12 gives tau~0.5 (T~60%). So in the orange-red the eclipse limb suppresses light by roughly a factor of 2-6, while in the blue-green (sigma falls to ~2-3e-22 at 450 nm) tau stays <0.1 (T>90%). That differential is the turquoise.

CONFIRMED IN REAL ECLIPSE SPECTRA: Pallé et al. 2009 (Nature 459, 814) recovered Earth's transmission spectrum from a lunar eclipse and the broad O3 Chappuis dip (0.375-0.650 um) dominates the visible; García Muñoz & Pallé (2011) and follow-ups model the eclipse 'effective' ozone column. Fosbury/Koch (ESO Messenger 143, 2011) show the same 560-600 nm Chappuis dip in pre-totality twilight and eclipse spectra. Vollmer & Gedzelman's ray-tracing reproduced the turquoise fringe and noted high-ozone arctic air can make one umbral edge the deepest blue.

GEOMETRY OF THE FRINGE: NASA GSFC (eclipse.gsfc.nasa.gov) - the troposphere+stratosphere act as a ring lens; thin high (stratospheric) layers refract through SMALL angles into the OUTER umbra, dense low layers refract through LARGE angles into the umbra CORE. So the outer ring of the umbra is preferentially stratosphere-lit (ozone-dominated, less tropospheric reddening) -> turquoise rim; the core is tropospheric-lit -> blood red.
reviews.physics_model_recommendation: Use a Beer-Lambert (pure transmission) model along refracted tangent rays, layered by tangent altitude. For each wavelength: T(lambda, h_tan) = exp(-tau), tau = sigma_O3(lambda) * N_O3_slant(h_tan) + tau_Rayleigh(h_tan,lambda) + tau_aerosol. Build N_O3_slant by integrating an ozone number-density profile (Gaussian/US-Standard, peak ~22 km, ~8e12 molecules/cm^3, layer 15-35 km) along the spherical-geometry chord for each tangent height; equivalently N_slant = AMF(h_tan) * N_vertical with AMF computed from spherical-shell chord geometry (path = 2*[sqrt(r_outer^2 - r_tan^2) - sqrt(r_inner^2 - r_tan^2)] summed over shells). Use sigma_O3 from a tabulated Chappuis cross-section (Brion-Daumont-Malicet or Serdyuchenko/IUP-Bremen; anchor to 5.2e-21 at 603 nm), 298 K is fine since T-dependence <3%. The umbral COLOR at a given point is the wavelength-weighted sum of all refracted rays whose tangent height maps (via differential refraction) to that umbral radius; you cannot get the turquoise rim without (a) refraction mapping high tangent heights to the outer umbra and (b) including Rayleigh extinction so the lower-tangent (red) rays are correctly attenuated. For a first-cut color curve, the minimal model is: compute T(lambda) at one representative 'fringe' tangent height (~10-20 km) with AMF~20-40, multiply by the solar spectrum, integrate against CIE color-matching functions to get the rim hue; do the same at a low tangent height (deep troposphere, large Rayleigh + aerosol) for the red core. SAFE approximations: pure absorption (no multiple scattering) for the first transmission; T-independent O3 cross-section; spherical-shell geometry. DANGEROUS approximations: plane-parallel/vertical-column geometry (kills the entire effect - the AMF amplification is the mechanism); ignoring refraction (it both bends rays into the umbra AND sets which tangent height illuminates which umbral radius); ignoring Rayleigh/aerosol (without tropospheric reddening you get blue everywhere, not a red core with turquoise rim); using only the 603 nm peak instead of the full band shape (the secondary 575 nm peak and the 500/650 nm shoulders set the exact hue).
pitfalls: Using the VERTICAL optical depth (tau~0.04) instead of the slant/limb optical depth (tau~1.5) - the vertical effect is invisible; the turquoise exists ONLY because of the ~12-45x air-mass enhancement of the tangent path. This is the single most common mistake.

Treating the Chappuis band as a single Lorentzian at 600 nm. It is a broad continuum with TWO maxima (575 and 603 nm) and asymmetric shoulders; the realistic hue depends on the full band shape from ~480 to ~680 nm, not just the peak.

Confusing the two color regimes: the RED umbra core is tropospheric Rayleigh/aerosol scattering, NOT ozone; the TURQUOISE rim is stratospheric ozone Chappuis absorption. Modeling only ozone gives blue everywhere; modeling only Rayleigh gives red everywhere. You need both plus refraction to separate them spatially.

Forgetting refraction's dual role: it bends rays into the shadow AND determines the mapping from tangent altitude to umbral radius. High thin stratospheric layers -> small refraction angle -> outer umbra (turquoise); dense low layers -> large refraction -> inner umbra (red). Drop this and the rim/core separation disappears.

Over-trusting a fixed AMF. The enhancement varies strongly with tangent height (sharply higher as the ray dips toward/below the ozone-layer peak) and with the assumed ozone profile width. Report a range (AMF ~12 to ~45, tau(603) ~0.5 to ~1.9) rather than a single value, and ideally integrate the real profile rather than using a slab.

Applying a large temperature correction to the cross-section - unnecessary here; Chappuis is nearly T-independent (<3%). Spending effort on T-dependence is misplaced; spend it on geometry and the band shape.

Ozone column is latitude/season dependent (~220 DU tropics to >400 DU high-latitude spring). High-ozone arctic air over one limb makes that umbral edge noticeably bluer (Vollmer & Gedzelman). Assuming a single uniform 300 DU misses real eclipse-to-eclipse and edge-to-edge variation.

Neglecting that scattered (not just directly transmitted) sunlight contaminates the umbra spectrum, which can inflate the apparent O3 column inferred from band ratios (García Muñoz & Pallé 2011, especially after volcanic aerosol loading). For a forward color model this matters less, but do not over-interpret observed band depths as pure transmission.
reviews.expected_quantitative_signature: Wavelengths/peak: Chappuis band ~400-650 nm (treat as significant ~480-680 nm), twin maxima at 575 nm and 602-603 nm; effective dip center in observed twilight/eclipse spectra ~585-600 nm. Peak cross-section: sigma(603)=5.23e-21 cm^2, sigma(575)=4.83e-21 cm^2 (T-independent to <3%); shoulders ~2e-21 at 500/650 nm, falling to a few e-22 by 450 nm. Columns: vertical 300 DU = 8.07e18 molecules/cm^2; tau_vertical(603)~0.042 (96% transmission - invisible). Limb path: tangent chord through the 15-35 km ozone layer ~600-700 km; AMF ~12 (high tangent) to ~35-45 (tangent near layer peak). Limb slant column ~1e20-3.6e20 molecules/cm^2 -> tau(603) ~0.5-1.9, i.e. orange-red transmission ~15-60%, while blue-green (450-500 nm) transmission stays >90% (tau<0.1). Net: roughly a 2-6x suppression of 600 nm light relative to 480 nm light along the fringe ray -> turquoise hue. SPATIALLY: turquoise appears as a thin rim at the OUTER edge of the umbra (lit by high, ozone-rich, low-Rayleigh stratospheric rays), grading inward to copper-red in the umbra core (tropospheric Rayleigh/aerosol reddening); best seen near U1/U2 and U3/U4 contacts. SOURCES: Wikipedia 'Chappuis absorption' (cross-sections, band range); JPL Publication Data Evaluation Sec.4 (cross-section datasets, band systems); Vollmer & Gedzelman, Applied Optics 47, H149 (2008) [definitive ray-tracing of eclipse color incl. turquoise fringe]; Pallé et al., Nature 459, 814 (2009) [eclipse transmission spectrum, Chappuis dip 0.375-0.650 um]; García Muñoz & Pallé (2011), García Muñoz et al. (2011) [eclipse RT models, effective O3 column]; Fosbury et al., ESO Messenger 143 (2011) and 178 (2019) [twilight/eclipse Chappuis spectra]; NASA GSFC eclipse.gsfc.nasa.gov/LEcat5/appearance.html [ring-lens refraction geometry]; NASA ozonewatch.gsfc.nasa.gov & Wikipedia 'Dobson unit' (300 DU, 2.69e16 molec/cm^2 per DU).
reviews.confidence: High on the cross-section magnitude, band range, vertical column, and the qualitative mechanism (all from primary/standard sources). Medium-high on the exact limb air-mass factor and resulting limb optical depth (AMF ~12-45, tau(603) ~0.5-1.9): these are my own spherical-shell geometric estimates anchored to standard ozone-layer altitudes, consistent with the literature's qualitative claims but not pulled verbatim from a single eclipse paper - the precise value depends on tangent height and ozone profile and should be computed per-ray in the actual model.

reviews.angle: Tangent-height-to-color mapping for sunlight refracted into Earth's umbra during a lunar eclipse, treated as a limb-sounding / transmission-spectrum problem: the umbra rim is illuminated by rays whose perigee (tangent height) sits in the ozone-dominated stratosphere (turquoise/blue), while the umbra interior is fed by rays grazing the dense lower troposphere where Rayleigh lambda^-4 extinction has stripped everything but red. Refraction is the enabling mechanism (it bends light into the geometric shadow at all), and the slant/air-mass enhancement along a tangent ray (~tens) is what makes both the reddening and the ozone absorption visually dominant.
key_findings: WHY LIGHT ENTERS THE UMBRA (refraction). A sea-level-grazing tangential ray is refracted by ~70 arcminutes (~1.17 deg), the same bending as the setting Sun seen from the ground but applied a second time on exit at the opposite limb. Mallama (2021) computes that this deflects the ray by ~7800 km at the lunar distance, an amount LARGER than Earth's radius. That deflection is exactly what fills the geometric umbra cone (whose half-angle is only ~0.25 deg narrower than geometric) with light. Standard refraction at the astronomical horizon is ~34-35 arcmin per single limb crossing; the umbra-filling ray crosses the limb effectively twice, so total bending is ~1 to 1.2 deg. Refraction is nearly wavelength-independent (color comes from extinction, not from the bending itself).

REFRACTION SCALES WITH DENSITY -> ALTITUDE SORTS THE UMBRA RADIALLY. Bending angle is proportional to local air density along the ray's perigee. A surface ray bends ~70'; a ray with perigee at 50 km bends only ~1/1000 as much (~8 km deflection vs ~8000 km). Consequence: low tangent height (dense air, large bending) maps to the INNER/central umbra; high tangent height (thin stratospheric air, small bending) maps to the OUTER umbra near the rim. This is the geometric backbone of the color gradient.

TANGENT HEIGHT -> COLOR. Low tangent height (~0-12 km, troposphere): the slant path runs through the densest air, Rayleigh scattering (sigma ~ lambda^-4) removes blue/green ~16x more strongly than red, leaving DEEP COPPER-RED. This light fills the umbra center/inner region. Higher tangent height (~stratosphere, roughly 20-40 km): far less Rayleigh column, so the residual beam is much bluer, but it now passes through the bulk of the ozone column. Ozone's Chappuis band absorbs the orange-red (peak near 575-603 nm) while leaving a transparency window in the blue (~450-500 nm), producing the TURQUOISE/BLUE rim seen at the edge of the umbra. Net: red center, turquoise edge, with the transition set by tangent height.

OZONE CHAPPUIS BAND (the turquoise agent). Broad visible absorption ~400-650 nm with twin maxima at 575 nm (sigma ~4.8e-21 cm^2) and 603 nm (sigma ~5.2e-21 cm^2), centered ~600 nm. It removes yellow-orange-red and leaves a relative transmission peak in the blue. Because the rim ray has a LONG slant path through the ozone layer (ozone peaks ~20-30 km; eclipse spectroscopy localizes the effective absorbing layer toward higher altitudes), the Chappuis optical depth becomes large enough to flip the residual color from straw/red to blue-green. Garcia Munoz & Palle (2011) and lunar-eclipse transmission-spectrum work (Vidal-Madjar/Palle 2014, arXiv:1405.4780) identify Chappuis as the single most prominent feature in the eclipsed-Moon spectrum.

SLANT PATH / AIR MASS FACTOR (~tens). A vertical ray = 1 air mass. A ray tangent at the horizon traverses ~38 air masses (standard astronomical value at zenith angle 90 deg). For the umbra geometry, where light grazes the limb on BOTH entry and exit, the effective slant enhancement is of order 2x38 ~ several tens. This ~30-40x amplification is why both Rayleigh reddening and ozone Chappuis absorption, which are minor for a vertical path, become totally dominant: e.g. Mallama's model has blue light at the umbra center down ~22 magnitudes (factor ~1e9), red far less, purely from the air-mass-weighted color-dependent extinction.

STANDARD APPROXIMATIONS. (1) Extinction-only / single-scattering: the refracted beam is treated as a pencil that loses photons by molecular Rayleigh scattering + ozone (and aerosol) absorption along its slant path; light scattered INTO the beam is neglected. This is the basis of Mallama-type models (which even omit ozone in the base model) and is the standard for first-order color prediction. (2) Refraction computed via density-proportional bending with an isothermal scale height H~8 km (low atmosphere). (3) Spherical-shell, horizontally homogeneous atmosphere (Beer-Lambert along the curved ray). Single-scattering + extinction-only is adequate for the qualitative red-center/turquoise-rim structure; it is DANGEROUS for absolute brightness near mid-totality and for the deep umbra, where multiple scattering, focusing/de-focusing by the atmospheric lens, aerosol (volcanic) loading, and cloud/terrain blocking at low tangent heights matter (Garcia Munoz & Palle do full multiple-scattering radiative transfer for this reason).
reviews.physics_model_recommendation: Model the umbra as a limb-transmission problem. For each tangent (perigee) height h, trace a refracted ray through a spherical-shell atmosphere: bending angle alpha(h) proportional to local density rho(h) ~ rho0*exp(-h/H), H~8 km low / ~6-7 km in stratosphere; calibrate so alpha(0) ~ 70 arcmin (~0.0204 rad). The total deflection at lunar distance d_moon (~3.6e5 km) is roughly alpha*d_moon; map this to radial position in the shadow (alpha(0) gives ~7800 km > Earth radius, filling the umbra; alpha(50km) gives ~8 km). Compute slant column along the curved ray; for tangent geometry use the Chapman/air-mass enhancement (~38x vertical at the surface tangent, scaling down with h). Apply Beer-Lambert extinction tau(lambda,h) = tau_Rayleigh + tau_ozone + tau_aerosol: Rayleigh sigma_R(lambda) ~ lambda^-4 (sigma ~ 4.5e-27 cm^2 at 500 nm scaling), ozone via Chappuis cross-section sigma_O3(lambda) (twin peaks 575/603 nm ~5e-21 cm^2, window in blue) times slant ozone column (vertical ~300 DU = 8.1e18 molec/cm^2, times air-mass-like enhancement ~20-40 for tangent rays). Transmitted spectrum T(lambda,h) = exp(-tau) gives the color at radial position r(h). Use a single-scattering/extinction-only forward model for the qualitative color-vs-radius curve. For quantitative absolute intensity, especially the deep umbra and the brightness drop into totality, upgrade to full multiple-scattering radiative transfer (Garcia Munoz & Palle 2011 approach) and include atmospheric focusing and a realistic aerosol/cloud layer. Convolve transmitted spectra with eye/RGB or Johnson-Cousins B/V/R response to predict the visual turquoise-to-red transition.
pitfalls: Confusing single-limb sunset refraction (~34-35 arcmin) with the umbra-filling geometry: light reaching the Moon's shadow is bent at BOTH limbs, so total useful bending is ~1-1.2 deg and the slant air mass is roughly doubled relative to a single horizon ray. Using only ~35 arcmin / 38 air masses underestimates both the deflection and the extinction.

Treating refraction as wavelength-dependent for the color. Refraction is nearly achromatic; the color gradient is produced entirely by wavelength-dependent EXTINCTION (Rayleigh lambda^-4 + ozone Chappuis), not by chromatic bending. Mixing these up gives the wrong physical story.

Ignoring ozone. A Rayleigh-only model (or one that omits O3, like Mallama's base model) produces red but CANNOT produce the turquoise rim. The blue/turquoise is specifically the Chappuis absorption carving out orange-red and leaving the blue transmission window at high tangent heights. Omitting ozone is the single most common reason a simulated curve has no turquoise band.

Placing the ozone too low or as a thin slab. The effective absorbing layer inferred from eclipse spectroscopy sits high (ozone number-density peak ~20-30 km but slant-weighted effective contribution biased toward the stratosphere/upper layers); the turquoise appears precisely because the rim rays sample this layer with a long slant path. A near-surface ozone assumption mislocates the band.

Forgetting low-altitude blocking. At the lowest tangent heights (<~6-12 km) clouds, aerosols, and high terrain along Earth's limb physically block or strongly attenuate the reddest rays. This truncates the inner-umbra red contribution and makes eclipse brightness highly variable and event-specific (volcanic aerosol can dim by several tenths to a full magnitude or more).

Assuming the geometric umbra edge equals the optical edge. Refraction enlarges the shadow's reach but ozone Chappuis absorption COUNTERACTS the enlargement (it removes the very rays that would brighten the rim), so the observed umbra appears less enlarged than pure refraction predicts. The classic ~2% empirical umbral enlargement is a net of refraction plus absorption, not refraction alone.

Using extinction-only / single-scattering for absolute brightness in the deep umbra. It is fine for the color-vs-radius shape but underestimates light in the central umbra where multiple scattering and atmospheric focusing contribute; for a quantitative brightness (Danjon L value) curve you need full radiative transfer.
reviews.confidence: Medium-high on the qualitative physics, mechanisms, and the firmly-sourced anchor numbers (70 arcmin bending and 7800 km deflection from Mallama; Chappuis 575/603 nm and ~5e-21 cm^2 from Wikipedia/spectropol; ~38 horizon air mass standard; ozone confined-layer altitudes). Medium on the exact tangent-height boundaries of the turquoise vs red band (I give ~0-12 km troposphere = red and ~20-40 km stratosphere = turquoise as physically-reasoned ranges; published work gives the mechanism and altitude regimes but a single canonical crossover altitude in km is not cleanly stated in the sources I read and is itself event/ozone-column dependent). The factor-of-two double-limb air-mass argument is my physical reasoning consistent with the sources, not a directly quoted number.
reviews.expected_quantitative_signature: Refraction: surface-grazing tangential ray bent ~70 arcmin (~1.17 deg); deflection at lunar distance ~7800 km (> Earth radius), which is what fills the umbra. Bending scales with density: ray at 50 km tangent height bends ~1000x less (~8 km deflection). Scale height H ~ 8 km. Slant path: vertical = 1 air mass; horizon-tangent ray ~38 air masses; umbra double-limb geometry ~ several tens (~30-70). Rayleigh: sigma ~ lambda^-4, so blue (~450 nm) scattered ~ (700/450)^4 ~ 5.9x more than red (700 nm); over tens of air masses this is a multi-magnitude color split (Mallama: blue at umbra center down ~22 mag ~ factor 1e9, red far less). Ozone Chappuis: band 400-650 nm, twin maxima 575 nm (sigma ~4.83e-21 cm^2) and 603 nm (sigma ~5.23e-21 cm^2), centered ~600 nm, with a blue transmission window ~450-500 nm. Vertical ozone column ~300 DU = 8.1e18 molec/cm^2; tau_O3,vertical ~ 0.04 at 600 nm, but x(~20-40 slant enhancement) -> tau_O3 ~ 0.8-1.6, enough to visibly redden-out the orange and leave a turquoise residual. TANGENT-HEIGHT-TO-COLOR: ~0-12 km perigee -> deep copper-red (Rayleigh-dominated), feeding inner umbra; ~20-40 km perigee -> turquoise/blue (Chappuis-dominated), feeding the outer umbra rim. Ozone inferred (eclipse spectroscopy) to be effectively confined to a high layer (~50-80 km in older retrievals; modern peak density ~20-30 km). Sources: Mallama 2021 (arXiv:2112.08966); Garcia Munoz & Palle 2011 (J. Quant. Spectrosc. Radiat. Transfer, ScienceDirect S0022407311001361); Palle/Vidal-Madjar et al. 2014 lunar-eclipse Earth transmission spectrum (arXiv:1405.4780); Wikipedia 'Chappuis absorption' and 'Air mass (astronomy)'; NASA/EclipseWise 'Visual Appearance of Lunar Eclipses'; spectropol.pl ozone-at-twilight; arXiv:1205.5167 (stratosphere retrieval from eclipsed Moon).

reviews.angle: Color science pipeline (spectrum -> XYZ -> sRGB) for the turquoise band, with emphasis on the normalization choice that separates per-curve hue from absolute extinction-driven luminance.
key_findings: The standard pipeline in the RFC is correct and is the industry-standard spectral-rendering path: XYZ = integral I(lambda) * CMF(lambda) dlambda over 380-780nm, then linear sRGB = M * XYZ, then sRGB gamma encode. Validated against pbrt (pbr-book.org Color chapter), Scratchapixel, momentsingraphics 'Spectral rendering part 1', and the Python colour-science library. Use Riemann/trapezoid sum: X = sum_lambda I(lambda)*xbar(lambda)*dlambda (same for Y with ybar, Z with zbar).

CRITICAL normalization decision for this problem: there are TWO independent normalizations and they must be kept separate. (1) The CMF normalization constant k. (2) Whether you renormalize per tangent height. For comparing colors across tangent heights where absolute brightness must drop into the umbra, you must NOT renormalize per-curve. Compute k ONCE from the unattenuated source spectrum at the bright/high-altitude limb (effectively the incident solar spectrum, set so its Y maps to ~1.0 = reference white luminance), then apply that SAME k to every tangent height. Then Y(h) automatically carries the real extinction (Y drops by orders of magnitude into the umbra) while hue is still read off the chromaticity. This is exactly the 'k computed from illuminant once, reused for all samples' pattern, distinct from the colour-science default sd_to_XYZ which normalizes each SD to its own illuminant (relative colorimetry) and would destroy your luminance ladder.

Hue extraction: convert XYZ to CIELAB relative to a fixed white point (Xn,Yn,Zn = your unattenuated source / D65-like white, the SAME reference used for normalization), then hue angle h = atan2(b*, a*) in degrees (add 360 if negative), chroma C* = sqrt(a*^2 + b*^2). a* is red(+)/green(-), b* is yellow(+)/blue(-). For the turquoise band you expect a* < 0 (greenish) and b* < 0 (bluish) so h lands in the ~180-240 deg quadrant; blood-red region has a*>0, b*>0, h near 30-60 deg. Reporting hue in CIELAB (perceptually uniform) is better than HSV-on-sRGB because sRGB hue is non-uniform and breaks for out-of-gamut/clipped colors. Luminance: report Y directly (relative to white Y=1), or L* = 116*f(Y/Yn)-16 if you want a perceptual lightness curve, but for 'real extinction' Y on a log axis is the honest physical quantity.

Luminance Y is literally the photometric luminance because the CIE ybar CMF equals the photopic luminous efficiency V(lambda). So Y(h) directly is the relative brightness profile and will span many decades from white limb to deep umbra. Plot Y(h) on a log axis. Do NOT clamp Y into [0,1] before plotting the physical curve; clamping is only for the final 8-bit display render, and there you need an explicit exposure/tone-map decision (a single global exposure scale applied to all heights, NOT per-pixel auto-exposure, or the umbra-vs-limb brightness contrast that defines the phenomenon vanishes).

Gamut clipping is a real hazard precisely because turquoise and deep red are highly saturated near the spectral locus, so linear sRGB will frequently go negative or >1. Naive per-channel clip (clamp each of R,G,B to [0,1]) shifts hue and is the single most common bug that would make your hue curve wrong. Recommended: clip toward the white point at constant luminance/hue (desaturate just enough to enter gamut) rather than per-channel clamp. References: bottosson.github.io/posts/gamutclipping, coloraide gamut docs, momentsingraphics. Track and report the fraction of out-of-gamut samples; if large, consider rendering the hue analysis in a wider space (Rec.2020 or work in XYZ/Lab) and only clip at the very last display step. The hue/Lab curve should be computed BEFORE any gamut clipping.

Authoritative physics+color reference: Garcia Munoz & Pesnell, 'Simulating irradiance and color during lunar eclipses using satellite data' (Applied Optics 47(34):H149, 2008, opg.optica.org/abstract.cfm?uri=ao-47-34-h149) does exactly ray-trace + Rayleigh + ozone + aerosol -> color and is the gold-standard cross-check for your hue(h) and Y(h) curves. The turquoise band is driven by ozone Chappuis absorption: continuum 400-650nm, peak cross-section 5.23e-21 cm^2 at ~603nm, secondary 4.83e-21 cm^2 at ~575nm (Chappuis absorption, Wikipedia).
reviews.physics_model_recommendation: Pipeline equations: (1) Tristimulus by numerical integration over a uniform lambda grid (380-780nm, dlambda=1-5nm; 1nm preferred for matching CIE spec and resolving the spectral-locus colors of saturated turquoise/red). X = k*sum I(lambda)*xbar(lambda)*dlambda; Y,Z analogous. (2) Normalization: compute k = 1 / (sum I_ref(lambda)*ybar(lambda)*dlambda) ONCE, where I_ref is the unattenuated incident solar spectrum (your bright limb). Reuse this single k at every tangent height. This makes Y_ref = 1 (white) and Y(h) the physically correct relative luminance carrying full extinction. (3) XYZ -> linear sRGB with the standard D65 matrix R = 3.2406*X -1.5372*Y -0.4986*Z; G = -0.9689*X +1.8758*Y +0.0415*Z; B = 0.0557*X -0.2040*Y +1.0570*Z. (4) Gamut: do hue/Lab analysis on un-clipped linear values; for display, gamut-map by desaturating toward white at fixed luminance, then (5) gamma encode: c_srgb = 12.92*c if c<=0.0031308 else 1.055*c^(1/2.4)-0.055, then clamp [0,1] and *255. For hue/luminance CURVES (the L0 deliverable): convert XYZ->CIELAB with fixed white = I_ref XYZ; report h=atan2(b*,a*) deg, C*, and Y (log axis). Safe approximations: single-scattering limb transmission, treating Rayleigh as pure extinction; 5772K blackbody for I_sun is fine for hue trends (the differential ozone/Rayleigh shaping dominates over solar-spectrum detail). DANGEROUS approximations: (a) per-curve renormalization of Y -> kills the umbra brightness drop; (b) per-channel clamp before reading hue -> corrupts the hue curve for saturated colors; (c) auto-exposing each pixel/height independently in the final render -> destroys the red-center/turquoise-ring/white-edge luminance contrast; (d) coarse lambda grid (>5nm) under-resolves the narrow Chappuis-shaped transmitted spectrum and the spectral-locus chromaticity.
reviews.expected_quantitative_signature: Hue trajectory along increasing tangent height: deep umbra blood-red at CIELAB hue ~25-50 deg (a*>0,b*>0, dominant wavelength ~610-640nm), transitioning through the turquoise band at hue ~190-220 deg (a*<0 greenish, b*<0 bluish; dominant/complementary wavelength in the cyan ~480-500nm region), converging to near-white/neutral (low chroma, h undefined) at the high-altitude limb. The turquoise appears in the height window where the line-of-sight tangent passes through the stratospheric ozone layer (~roughly 20-40km tangent heights) where Rayleigh optical depth has dropped enough that blue is no longer fully scattered out but ozone Chappuis still removes orange-red. Chappuis cross-section to use: 5.23e-21 cm^2 peak at 603nm, 4.83e-21 cm^2 at 575nm, continuum 400-650nm (Chappuis absorption, Wikipedia); for the full sigma_o3(lambda) curve use Serdyuchenko et al. 2014 (already noted in your PRD). Luminance Y(h): expect a drop of several orders of magnitude (roughly 1e3-1e5x, eclipse umbra is ~1e4-1e5 dimmer than full moon) from limb to deep umbra; plot on log axis. Cross-check magnitudes and color against Garcia & Pesnell AO 2008 (ao-47-34-h149). CIE CMF DATA SOURCES: primary = CVRL, http://cvrl.ioo.ucl.ac.uk/cmfs.htm and http://www.cvrl.org/database/text/cmfs/ciexyz31.htm (CIE 1931 2-deg xbar,ybar,zbar at 1nm and 5nm, ASCII/CSV, equals Wyszecki & Stiles Table I(3.3.1)); easiest programmatic = Python colour-science colour.MSDS_CMFS['CIE 1931 2 Degree Standard Observer'] (and colour.SDS_ILLUMINANTS['D65']); R colorSpec package objects xyz1931.1nm / xyz1931.5nm.
pitfalls: Per-curve luminance renormalization (the colour-science sd_to_XYZ default behavior) erases the umbra brightness drop. Compute k once from the bright-limb/incident spectrum and reuse it; do not let each tangent height define its own white.

Per-channel sRGB clamp before extracting hue corrupts hue for saturated turquoise/red. Do the Lab/hue analysis on unclipped linear values; gamut-map by desaturation toward white at the final display stage only.
Forgetting sRGB gamma (the piecewise OETF with 1/2.4 and the 12.92 linear toe). Plotting linear values as if display-ready makes everything look too dark and shifts apparent hue/saturation.
Using HSV-on-sRGB for the hue curve. sRGB-space hue is perceptually non-uniform and ill-defined once values clip; use CIELAB hue angle instead.

Mismatched white points between normalization and the CIELAB reference. The Xn,Yn,Zn used in Lab MUST equal the same incident-spectrum white used to set k, otherwise hue and lightness are referenced inconsistently.
Auto-exposing the final render per height/pixel. The phenomenon IS the luminance contrast (dim red center, brighter turquoise ring, bright white edge); apply one global exposure for the whole disk.

Coarse wavelength grid (>5nm) under-resolves both the Chappuis-shaped transmitted spectrum and the spectral-locus chromaticity of highly saturated colors; use 1nm and integrate consistently (Riemann/trapezoid with explicit dlambda).
Confusing 2-degree vs 10-degree observer: use CIE 1931 2-degree (ciexyz31) for a small object like the eclipsed moon, not the 1964 10-degree set.

Treating Rayleigh as absorption vs extinction inconsistently: in the single-scattering limb model Rayleigh is an extinction (loss) term in tau; this is the intended approximation but should be stated, since multiple scattering would partially refill the blue.
reviews.confidence: High. The pipeline (spectrum->CMF integral->XYZ->sRGB), the CIELAB hue-angle math, the gamut-clipping hazard, and the CMF data sources are all confirmed across multiple authoritative references (pbrt, momentsingraphics, CVRL, colour-science, Wikipedia CIELAB/Chappuis). The normalization recommendation (single k from incident spectrum, no per-curve renorm) is a direct consequence of the stated requirement and standard absolute colorimetry; the Garcia & Pesnell AO 2008 paper is the domain-specific validation reference. Exact tangent-height numbers for the turquoise window are order-of-magnitude estimates and should be pinned by your own radiative-transfer run and cross-checked against that paper.

dataSources.data_type: Ozone (O3) absorption cross-sections covering the Chappuis band (visible, ~400-800 nm) and beyond, as a function of temperature. Full spectral coverage 213-1100 nm at 0.01 nm grid. This is the Serdyuchenko/Gorshelev/Weber/Burrows 2014 dataset (the community gold standard, e.g. ACSO-recommended).
dataSources.best_source: Serdyuchenko-Gorshelev UV/VIS/NIR ozone absorption cross-section dataset, IUP Bremen MolSpec Lab (Serdyuchenko A., Gorshelev V., Weber M., Burrows J.P.), archived on Zenodo (DOI 10.5281/zenodo.5793207). This is the same data distributed via the IUP Bremen UVSAT atlas and the MPI-Mainz UV/VIS Spectral Atlas.
dataSources.format_and_units: Plain-text ASCII .dat file (12.5 MB, CRLF line endings), 45-line text header followed by 88,668 whitespace-delimited data rows. 12 columns. Column 1 = vacuum wavelength in nm. Columns 2-12 = absorption cross section in cm^2/molecule (scientific notation, e.g. 7.68453E-19) at 11 temperatures: 293, 283, 273, 263, 253, 243, 233, 223, 213, 203, 193 K (in that column order). Wavelength grid: uniform 0.01 nm spacing from 213.000 to 1100.000 nm. Native spectral resolution (HWHM) varies by region: 0.01 nm below 290 nm and 350-450 nm; 1 cm^-1 elsewhere; all interpolated onto the 0.01 nm grid. Stated total relative systematic uncertainty <3%.
dataSources.citation: Gorshelev, V., Serdyuchenko, A., Weber, M., Chehade, W., and Burrows, J. P.: High spectral resolution ozone absorption cross-sections - Part 1: Measurements, data analysis and comparison with previous measurements around 293 K, Atmos. Meas. Tech., 7, 609-624, doi:10.5194/amt-7-609-2014, 2014. AND Serdyuchenko, A., Gorshelev, V., Weber, M., Chehade, W., and Burrows, J. P.: High spectral resolution ozone absorption cross-sections - Part 2: Temperature dependence, Atmos. Meas. Tech., 7, 625-636, doi:10.5194/amt-7-625-2014, 2014. Dataset DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.5793207 (CC-BY-4.0).
dataSources.notes: Verified by downloading the file directly (curl succeeded, 12 MB) and inspecting both header and data rows in /tmp/ozone_serd.dat. The single file contains all 11 temperatures, so no per-temperature files are needed; the requested stratospheric (~223-253 K) and 293 K columns are all present, fully covering the Chappuis band (400-800 nm) and the wider 350-830 nm request. Temperature dependence is explicit (one column per temperature), not a parameterized fit. Wavelengths are VACUUM nm; convert to air wavelength if matching air-referenced spectrographs. File uses CRLF endings and ' ' (space) decimal separators in scientific notation; skip the 45-line header (stated as 'NUMBER OF HEADER LINES: 45') when parsing. Alternative access points for the identical data: IUP Bremen UVSAT data page https://www.iup.uni-bremen.de/UVSAT/data/xsection and the MPI-Mainz UV/VIS Spectral Atlas (https://uv-vis-spectral-atlas-mainz.org). Older alternatives mentioned in the task (Bogumil et al. 2003 GOME/SCIAMACHY FM, Burrows et al. 1999 GOME) are also on the MPI-Mainz atlas but have lower resolution and fewer/different temperatures; Serdyuchenko 2014 supersedes them for the Chappuis band. A '5digits' filename variant indicates 5 significant figures in the cross-section values.

dataSources.data_type: Atmospheric vertical profiles: (1) air (total) number density vs altitude per US Standard Atmosphere 1976; (2) ozone (O3) volume mixing ratio / number density vs altitude per AFGL standard atmosphere "US Standard" (model 6). Altitude coverage 0-100 km (CSV table extends to 120 km).
dataSources.best_source: AFGL Atmospheric Constituent Profiles (1986), "US Standard" model — machine-readable mirror in the rayference/afgl1986 GitHub repo (also bundled in the `joseki` PyPI package). For the pure air-density US Standard Atmosphere 1976 model, the authoritative computational source is the `ussa1976` PyPI package (NASA-TM-X-74335).
dataSources.download_url: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rayference/afgl1986/main/tables/1f.csv  (US Standard = AFGL table 1f / model 6; columns z,p,t,n,H2O,O3,N2O,CO,CH4). Other models: 1a Tropical, 1b Midlat Summer, 1c Midlat Winter, 1d Subarctic Summer, 1e Subarctic Winter, 1f US Standard. | Air-density model package: `pip install ussa1976` (https://pypi.org/project/ussa1976/ ; https://github.com/rayference/ussa1976). | Alt ASCII source: libRadtran data/atmmod/afglus.dat (download libRadtran 2.0.x from http://www.libradtran.org/doku.php?id=download). | Authoritative AFGL source PDF: https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA175173.pdf ; USSA1976 PDF: https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/ADA035728.pdf (NASA mirror https://ntrs.nasa.gov/api/citations/19780023689/downloads/19780023689.pdf).
dataSources.format_and_units: afgl1986 1f.csv: plain CSV, 51 rows (0-120 km), header z,p,t,n,H2O,O3,N2O,CO,CH4. Units: z = geometric altitude [km]; p = pressure [hPa/mbar]; t = temperature [K]; n = total air NUMBER DENSITY [cm^-3] (e.g. 2.548e19 at sea level); H2O,O3,N2O,CO,CH4 = volume mixing ratio [ppmv]. To get ozone number density: n_O3 [cm^-3] = O3[ppmv] * 1e-6 * n[cm^-3] (O3 peaks ~10 ppmv near 35 km; n=3.83e17 at 30 km). Altitude steps: 1 km (0-25), 2.5/5 km higher up. — ussa1976 package: import ussa1976; ds = ussa1976.compute(z=np.arange(0,100001,1)) returns an xarray.Dataset on a custom altitude grid in METERS; variable `n` = number density [m^-3] plus per-species number densities, temperature [K], pressure [Pa], etc., valid 0-1000 km.
dataSources.citation: Anderson, G.P., Clough, S.A., Kneizys, F.X., Chetwynd, J.H., Shettle, E.P. (1986). AFGL Atmospheric Constituent Profiles (0-120 km). AFGL-TR-86-0110, Environmental Research Papers No. 954, Air Force Geophysics Laboratory, Hanscom AFB, MA (DTIC ADA175173). | COESA (1976). U.S. Standard Atmosphere, 1976. NOAA/NASA/USAF, NOAA-S/T 76-1562 (also NASA-TM-X-74335; DTIC ADA035728). | ussa1976 Python package, Rayference (MIT), https://github.com/rayference/ussa1976.
dataSources.notes: Verified live: GitHub API listed tables 1a-2d as CSV; raw fetch of tables/1f.csv returned HTTP 200 with the expected header and sea-level air number density 2.548e19 cm^-3 and O3 ~10 ppmv around 35 km, confirming model 6 = US Standard. Caveats: (1) The afgl1986 CSV gives ozone as a VMR (ppmv); convert to number density by multiplying by total n in the same row — both columns are in the same file, so no external coupling needed, but note n here is the AFGL US-Standard density, which is essentially the USSA-1976 air density and consistent to use for the conversion. (2) AFGL profiles are time/season-invariant standard atmospheres (no temperature/diurnal dependence); the "US Standard" column is the climatological mean. (3) The standalone `ussa1976` package is now UNMAINTAINED (since 2025-07-29) but functionally folded into `joseki` (pip install joseki), which also ships the same AFGL-1986 profiles as CF-convention NetCDF readable with xarray — preferable if you want one package for both air density and ozone. (4) libRadtran afglus.dat is the most widely cited ASCII form of the same US-Standard profile but requires downloading/extracting the full libRadtran tarball (no stable single-file raw URL). (5) DTIC/NTRS PDFs are the original authoritative tables but are not machine-readable without OCR.

dataSources.data_type: Extraterrestrial (top-of-atmosphere / AM0) solar spectral irradiance reference spectrum, 380-780 nm
dataSources.best_source: ASTM E-490-00 AM0 Reference Spectrum (distributed by NREL). High-resolution alternative: SAO2010 / Chance-Kurucz 2010 solar reference spectrum (Harvard-Smithsonian CfA).
dataSources.download_url: PRIMARY (ASTM E-490, native W/m^2/nm): https://www.nrel.gov/grid/data/assets/data/e490_00a_amo.xls  (landing page: https://www.nrel.gov/grid/solar-resource/spectra-astm-e490.html). HIGH-RES ALTERNATIVE (SAO2010, raw ASCII file, confirmed live): http://www.cfa.harvard.edu/atmosphere/links/sao2010.solref.converted (landing page: https://lweb.cfa.harvard.edu/atmosphere/ ; paper: https://lweb.cfa.harvard.edu/atmosphere/publications/Chance-Kurucz-solar2010-JQSRT.pdf).
dataSources.format_and_units: ASTM E-490 (e490_00a_amo.xls): two-column Excel table, wavelength (micrometers) vs spectral irradiance in W/m^2/nm, AM0 / 1 AU; integrated solar constant 1366.1 W/m^2; spectral range 119.5 nm - 1,000,000 nm. Sampling in the 380-780 nm visible band is coarse (~1 nm steps). Units already in the requested W/m^2/nm. --- SAO2010 (sao2010.solref.converted): two-column whitespace-delimited ASCII, vacuum wavelength (nm) vs irradiance. Range 200.07-1000.99 nm, sampled at 0.01 nm, 0.04 nm FWHM resolution -- fully covers 380-780 nm at high resolution. NATIVE UNITS ARE photons/cm^2/s/nm (NOT W/m^2/nm); convert with E = (h*c/lambda) per photon, i.e. W/m^2/nm = photons/cm^2/s/nm * (h*c/lambda) * 1e4. (The accompanying sao2010.solref.air variant gives air wavelengths.)
dataSources.citation: ASTM E490-00a(2019), Standard Solar Constant and Zero Air Mass Solar Spectral Irradiance Tables, ASTM International, West Conshohocken, PA; spectrum distributed by NREL (formerly RReDC). | K. Chance and R.L. Kurucz, "An improved high-resolution solar reference spectrum for earth's atmosphere measurements in the ultraviolet, visible, and near infrared," Journal of Quantitative Spectroscopy and Radiative Transfer, 111(9), 1289-1295, 2010, doi:10.1016/j.jqsrt.2010.01.036.
dataSources.confidence: High on source identity, units, ranges and the SAO2010 direct file URL (the cfa download link was confirmed live via WebFetch of the cfa atmosphere landing page; SAO2010 native photon units confirmed via Chance-Kurucz paper and the sasktran/usask documentation). Medium on the exact current NREL asset path: the canonical filename e490_00a_amo.xls is well established and cross-confirmed, but NREL blocked automated HEAD/GET from this environment, so verify the exact /grid/data/assets/data/ path (or use the NREL landing page, which always links the current file) before scripting an automated download.
dataSources.notes: Choosing between the two: pick ASTM E-490 if you want the requested W/m^2/nm units with zero conversion and only need ~1 nm resolution across 380-780 nm; it is the aerospace-standard AM0 spectrum (solar constant 1366.1 W/m^2). Pick SAO2010 if you need fine spectral structure (Fraunhofer lines) at 0.01 nm sampling -- but you must convert photons/cm^2/s/nm to W/m^2/nm and be aware its absolute radiometric scale is tied to the Thuillier et al. spectrum (better than ~5% above 305 nm), and that it uses vacuum wavelengths. Both are top-of-atmosphere/1 AU (no atmospheric absorption); neither is an AM1.5 terrestrial spectrum. If you instead need a measured, time-varying modern TOA SSI in W/m^2/nm, the LASP TSIS-1 HSRS and NOAA NCEI SSI CDR are alternatives (both natively W/m^2/nm) but are observational climate records rather than a single fixed reference spectrum. For most fixed-reference 380-780 nm needs, ASTM E-490 is the simplest unit-exact answer and SAO2010 the high-resolution upgrade.

dataSources.data_type: CIE 1931 2-degree standard colorimetric observer color matching functions xbar ybar zbar
dataSources.best_source: CVRL (Colour & Vision Research Laboratory, Institute of Ophthalmology, UCL) database, the de facto authoritative online distributor of CIE colorimetric data
dataSources.format_and_units: Plain CSV, no header row. Four comma-separated columns per line: wavelength_nm, xbar, ybar, zbar. Wavelength in nanometers; xbar/ybar/zbar dimensionless tristimulus weighting values to ~12 significant figures. 5nm steps. File covers 360-830nm (93 rows, 4748 bytes); requested 380-780nm is a contiguous subset (filter 380<=col1<=780 gives 81 rows). Verified: row 380 = 0.001368, 0.000039, 0.006450; ybar peaks at exactly 1.000000 at 555nm.
dataSources.citation: CIE (1932). Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage Proceedings 1931, Cambridge University Press. CIE 1931 2-degree standard colorimetric observer. Redistributed via Stockman A. and Sharpe L.T., Colour and Vision Research Laboratory (CVRL), http://cvrl.org, file ciexyz31.csv.
dataSources.notes: Both URLs live and byte-identical: primary http://cvrl.ioo.ucl.ac.uk/database/data/cmfs/ciexyz31.csv and alias http://www.cvrl.org/database/data/cmfs/ciexyz31.csv (both HTTP 200, 4748 bytes). cvrl.org proxies to the ioo.ucl.ac.uk server. A 1nm version exists at ciexyz31_1.csv (HTTP 200, 23548 bytes, 471 rows); filter to 380-780nm for 401 rows. Caveats: (1) CMFs are not temperature- or illuminant-dependent, fixed observer functions, no temperature parameter applies. (2) Do not confuse with CIE 1964 10-degree observer (ciexyz64.csv) or CIE 2006/2015 cone-fundamental CMFs (separate files same server); the classic 1931 2-deg set is ciexyz31.csv. (3) Files have no header and may lack a trailing newline; parse defensively. (4) CVRL serves plain HTTP, HTTPS may not be available, do not force TLS.
